speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. Tell us about the picture please.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right, it's my mother, Golda Fuks Stollar, my grandmother, Chana [? Feiga ?] Fuks. [? Sarah ?] Fuks to the left. And on the horsey is Doba Fuks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother was 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken? In which--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tuczyn. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father and mother, Chanina Stollar and Golda Stollar Fuks.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken shortly after they were married, in Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me when I was six months old and my brother, a year and six months. His name was Mendl Stollar. And mine was Feigi Stollar. I was-- I was crying. My mother told me I was crying. So she gave me-- I have a chicken, a wooden chicken on my lap. [LAUGHS] My name is Feigi Gl-- Feigi Stollar, and my brother, Mendy. It was taken in year 929. And I'm sitting on the horsey, because I was spoiled. My brother wanted to sit there. But I was-- I'm sitting there. [LAUGHS] My brother Mendl and me. He was-- my brother was sick with typhus, so they had to cut him-- they had to cut his hair. He was a very, very gentle little boy. He was wonderful as a brother. He adored me, and I adored him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the writing, is this-- on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was taken in Tuczyn. And it was taken in '30-- '36. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the picture survived, because they sent it before the war to, to my grand-- to my grandfather. And that's why the picture survived. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? want to-- ?] my father, my father to the right, on the motorcycle with friends. And it, it was taken when he came to Cuba, not long after he was in Cuba. In what year, do you think?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early '30s do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early '30s. That's my Auntie Doba Fuks. She was like my sister, because she was 10 years younger than my mother. And my mother raised her till she got married. They loved her very much. I was very, very attached to her. When she got married, I cried. [LAUGHS] I really did. That's my Aunt Doba with the landlady in Warsaw. This is before she got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about the picture, the photographer? Do you want to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was saved in the States. My grandfather saved it in Hartford, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Aunt Doba and her husband [? Itzak ?] [INAUDIBLE]. He was-- because I didn't have a father, he was like a father to me. And they were killed in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Rowne, in '33. This picture is from Tuczyn from, from a school. And the-- I got it in Israel. And I, I like the picture because there is the teacher was my teacher too, when I was in the first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I say? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- was taken in Tuczyn. It was a Jewish school. And the teacher's name was [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. This picture was taken in Tuczyn. The organization is Gordonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got it in Israel, the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Gordonia? Do you want to talk a little bit about the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gordonia is just like Betar, like [NON-ENGLISH]. They all work-- everybody wants to go to Israel. But there were different names. Gordonia, [NON-ENGLISH]. But this was Gordonia. This picture is taken in Tuczyn. The organization was Betar. And I'm to the right. I must be there six years old. But the picture reminds me of so many things. Because we are-- they tried together-- the-- the organization, together-- everybody to they should-- we should know about Israel. And they teach us, they were teaching us that we should know that we have a place in the world. And some day we'll be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? That, that was the-- oh. To the right? To the-- I am to the right. I'm six years old. At the, at the bottom of the, of the picture, right? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael's bar mitzvah. My husband, Abram, Michael, myself, and Gary. Gary was at that time five years old. In '62, my husband gave me a present, a trip to Israel. So I went to Israel, into Jerusalem. And this was-- I went to the memorial for Tuczyn. It was very, very, very moving. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very moving. I, I-- after this picture was taken, I remember I had to see a doctor. That's my son, Gary, on his wedding day, with his bride, Bonnie Sandler. They were married in '80. And this, this is my son, Gary. He was a very talented man. I lost him in '88, eight years ago.
